results.justification: Hard fail on 'root cause' criterion: output contains a dedicated '## Root Cause' section with 'Root cause statement:' — the rubric explicitly fails any output where 'root cause' (singular) appears. Additional issues: (1) 'Preserving the Learning' section is entirely absent (only 5 of 6 required sections present, though this alone would be partial), (2) 'What We Learned' learnings do NOT use the required 'We now know that X, which means Y' format — they use descriptive paragraph format instead, (3) Forward Commitments (Action Items) lack required type tags (PREVENT/DETECT/MITIGATE/PROCESS) — they use High/Medium priority instead. The 'root cause' appearance alone triggers a fail per the rubric.

observations.text: Dedicated '## Root Cause' section with 'Root cause statement:' directly violates the no-singular-root-cause requirement — this is a hard fail criterion
'Preserving the Learning' section is completely absent; no concrete artifact (acceptance criterion template, Bird's domain model addition, Kobe checklist item) is called out in a dedicated section
Key Learnings do not use 'We now know that X, which means Y' format — they use paragraph descriptions (L1–L5) with different framing
Action Items lack type tags (PREVENT, DETECT, MITIGATE, PROCESS); uses Priority column (High/Medium) instead
4 contributing factors identified separately with clear explanations — meets the quantity and separation requirement

Tone is consistently non-prosecutorial — Shaq's fixture gap described as 'not because Shaq was careless,' Kobe's review described as a scope issue not negligence, Bird's exclusion framed as a classification/scoping decision
7 action items with named owners, well-specified and actionable — exceeds the 3-minimum even though type tags are missing
Situation Snapshot (as 'Incident Summary') covers all required elements: goal, agents involved, impact (847 transactions), duration (20 minutes), stale seasonal prices
Timeline reconstructed in experienced order with key events — though presented as a table rather than narrative, it covers the required sequence
Extra valuable sections ('What Went Right', 'Systemic Pattern to Watch') show depth of analysis beyond minimum requirements

| Time (UTC) | E

results.justification: All pass criteria met. Deprioritisation explicitly described as 'reasonable given the information available at the time' (CF-1). Four+ contributing factors clearly identified: deprioritisation in context (CF-1), sub-provider onboarding without notice (UC-2, timeline), silent-fail reconciliation (CF-3, UC-3), no assumption-staleness tracking (CF-2). Equivalent of 'given what we knew' framing present. No blame assigned to Bird, Shaq, or Coach K — Bird's flag called 'accurate and prescient', Shaq's implementation called 'faithful to the spec', Coach K's decision called 'reasonable'. Forward Commitments include A-6 (PROCESS action for re-evaluation triggers on accepted-risk items). What We Learned framed as durable systemic insights (L-1 through L-5), not blame. No 'should have known/validated' language. No 'ignored' framing for Bird's flag.

observations.text: CF-1 explicitly validates Coach K's decision as reasonable given available information, with 'given the information available at the time' framing
Contributing factors are rich and well-distributed: deprioritisation context, sub-provider onboarding, silent-fail reconciliation, no re-evaluation trigger, no format validation, no integration tests
Bird's flag consistently described as valuable domain analysis, never as 'ignored' — 'Bird's spec review was accurate and prescient' in What Went Well
Shaq explicitly exonerated: 'There was no implementation error. The failure was upstream of the code.'
A-6 directly addresses the assumption-tracking process gap: 'every deprioritised risk must have a re-evaluation trigger condition'
L-2 frames the lesson as systemic: 'accepted risk without a re-evaluation trigger is deferred certainty of harm' — no blame, just process insight
Summary statement 'None of these required new knowledge to prevent' borders on hindsight framing — while technically systemic, it could be read as implicit criticism of the team's decision-making

CF-2 notes 'The sprint commitment to address TD-291 in the next sprint was not kept — this is a process failure' which introduces mild accountability pressure, though it is correctly scoped to process rather than individuals
Section structure exceeds the 6-section minimum with 10 well-organized sections including Timeline, Root Cause Analysis, Contributing Factors, What Went Well, Lessons Learned, and Action Items

results.scenario_name: Scenario 23 — Learning Review No Single Root Cause
results.justification: Multiple hard fail criteria triggered: (1) 'root cause' appears in section title ('Root Cause Analysis') and body ('There is no single root cause'), directly violating the 'root cause (singular) completely absent' requirement. (2) 'Primary cause' appears in Section 6, explicitly listed as a forbidden phrase. (3) Section 6 introduces a proximate/enabling hierarchy ('Primary cause... proximate technical failure', 'Enabling cause'), directly contradicting the 'no hierarchy of factors' requirement. (4) No DETECT-tagged forward commitment exists — R-2 covers duplicate charge monitoring but is not tagged DETECT. (5) 'We now know that X, which means Y' format is absent — Section 10 uses declarative statements instead. The output does well on contributing factors (8 enumerated, exceeding the 5 minimum), 'What had to be true' framing is explicit, tone is non-blaming, and section count exceeds 6. But the presence of 'root cause' and 'primary cause' are automatic fail triggers per the rubric.

observations.text: 'root cause' appears in Section 6 title ('Root Cause Analysis') and body ('There is no single root cause') — rubric requires complete absence
'Primary cause' appears explicitly in Section 6, a forbidden phrase per expected behavior
Section 6 introduces proximate/enabling hierarchy ('proximate technical failure', 'Enabling cause'), directly violating the no-hierarchy requirement
No DETECT-tagged forward commitment — R-2 covers the right content but lacks the DETECT tag
What We Learned (Section 10) does not use 'We now know that X, which means Y' format — uses declarative bullet points instead
8 contributing factors enumerated separately, well exceeding the 5 minimum
'What had to be true' framing explicitly present in Contributing Factors header
Each contributing factor is described with clear independence — 'Remove any single one and the outcome does not occur'
No agent singled out as primarily responsible — tone distributes accountability across system conditions
Strong technical accuracy and depth throughout the review
The output contradicts itself: Section 4 says 'no single root cause' and factors are independent, then Section 6 explicitly ranks two factors as primary and enabling

results.justification: All pass criteria met. Preserving the Learning (Section 6) contains 6 named artifacts, each with type, detailed content description, and named owner. ADR-014 amendment owned by MJ (Artifact 5). Domain rule update with COMPLIANCE_TAGS.md owned by Bird (Artifact 2). Kobe review checklist update owned by Kobe (Artifact 1). Contributing Factors (Section 3) explicitly names Kobe's checklist gap (Gap 1) and the terminology mismatch (Section 2 entirely dedicated to it). Section 7 provides process-tagged forward commitments with owners and target dates. Tone is explicitly non-prosecutorial — Section 8 states 'Do not blame any individual' and frames the failure as systemic, not personal.

observations.text: Exceeds the 3-artifact minimum with 6 well-defined artifacts, each with specific file paths, content descriptions, and named owners
Section 2 isolates the terminology mismatch as the core finding with surgical precision — both definitions stated side-by-side
Section 8 explicitly prevents misreadings: no blame on MJ or Bird, no overreaction (don't eliminate event sourcing, don't add Bird to every ADR)
Artifact 5 (ADR-014 amendment) preserves the original decision and adds the correction — teaches rather than erases
Section 9 defines falsifiable success criteria for whether the learning stuck — goes beyond what the rubric required
Kobe's checklist gap is not just surfaced but codified as the first artifact with specific gate language
Bounded Context Compliance Sensitivity Register (Artifact 3) is a novel connective artifact between Bird's domain and MJ's architecture — demonstrates deep understanding of the structural gap
Section 7 (Process Changes) uses a table format without explicit PROCESS tags — the rubric asks for tagged commitments, though the content is clearly process-oriented
Clear distinction between Preserving the Learning (Section 6 — what to codify for future teams) and Process Changes (Section 7 — what to do now) avoids the conflation failure mode

results.justification: Exceptional substance on disagreement handling — both positions accurately attributed, no winner declared, disagreement explicitly framed as incomplete shared model signal. However, structural requirements for pass not met: missing 3 of the expected 6 named sections (What We Learned, Forward Commitments, Preserving the Learning). observations.text: Disagreement handling is exemplary — dedicated section titled 'A Signal, Not a Verdict' with both positions described in detail, attributed by name, and neither endorsed

Open Questions section adds genuine analytical depth — asks whether deadlock status was confirmed, what forward-fix would have cost
